text: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has popularized In-Context Learning (ICL) as a transformative paradigm in natural language processing. In this framework, models perform specialized tasks by leveraging a few-shot context rather than undergoing traditional weight updates. Given the growing importance of ICL in evaluating and extending the capabilities of LLMs, this paper provides a comprehensive survey of the current landscape, emerging progress, and persistent challenges in the field. We begin by establishing a formal definition of ICL and situating it within the broader context of related learning methodologies. We then categorize and evaluate state-of-the-art techniques, ranging from innovative training strategies to sophisticated prompt engineering and theoretical analyses. Furthermore, we explore a diverse array of application scenarios, including data engineering and dynamic knowledge updating. Finally, we highlight the inherent limitations of ICL and propose promising avenues for future investigation. Through this work, we aim to provide a foundational resource that inspires further research into the underlying mechanisms and optimization of In-Context Learning.
